1:
The chapter opens with a commission, and the witnesses gather first around the man Elisha sends and the manner of the sending. Who was this unnamed son of the prophets? John Gill, with Adam Clarke and Charles Ellicott, records the old Jewish tradition — Rashi's — that it was Jonah the son of Amittai, though none presses it as certain. Matthew Henry asks the sharper question of why Elisha delegated so honourable an errand rather than going himself, and answers on three counts: the prophet was too old and too well known to slip to Ramoth-gilead unobserved, the task carried danger (as Samuel felt when sent to anoint David, 1 Sam. 16:2), and it called for a young man's strength — "let youth work and age direct." Henry adds the gracious motive that Elisha "would not engross all the honours to himself," but let a young prophet share them.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the setting and the words. Keil and Delitzsch parse the orders exactly — gird the loins, take the flask of oil (as in 1 Sam. 10:1), seek out Jehu, draw him "into the innermost chamber," anoint, then "flee, and thou shalt not wait." That last command, they insist, was not to guard the young prophet from Ahab's secret partisans (so Theodoret and Clericus had supposed) but simply "that he might not mix himself up with other affairs." Ellicott catches a nuance the older versions blur: the Hebrew reads "meanwhile Elisha had called" — the anointing runs contemporaneously with Joram lying ill of his Ramoth wounds, not after it.

Two smaller disagreements surface. Henry weighs and rejects the notion that Jehu had been anointed privately once before by Elijah, as David was by Samuel; had that been so, he reasons, Elijah must have anointed Hazael too, whereas God plainly reserved these acts for Elisha to perform "when he was gone." And on the oil itself Henry offers a fine touch: Solomon was anointed with oil from the tabernacle (1 Kings 1:39); that could not now be had, "but oil from a prophet's hand was equivalent to oil out of God's house."

Beneath the details the voices are one. This is no palace coup hatched by ambition — Gill, Henry, and Keil agree that Jehu neither sought nor expected the throne; the commission "was a perfect surprise to him." The haste, the secrecy, the flask, the flight all mark it as an errand from "a higher king," and the sons of the prophets are commended, in Henry's phrase, to "be quick and lively at their work," like the angels that fly swiftly.
2:
The instructions continue, and here the grammatical-historical commentators do almost all the work, for Gill's note is brief. The single most-discussed point is the phrase rendered "when thou comest thither." Charles Ellicott argues it means rather "enter into it" — into the town of Ramoth itself — and draws a large inference: the Israelites must by now have retaken Ramoth from the Syrians, since the story goes on to speak of "chambers" and "the door" (v.3) and of letting "no man escape out of the city" (v.15). Josephus asserts as much. This quietly corrects any picture of a city still under siege.

On Jehu himself the witnesses assemble what little is known. Ellicott notes he was probably "left in supreme command of the forces at Jehoram's departure, as being the ablest of the generals," and remarks the irony that the father of the man who executes Jehovah's sentence bore the name Jehoshaphat, "Jehovah judgeth." He then reaches outside Scripture altogether, to the Assyrian monuments: Jehu is twice named by Shalmaneser II as a tributary — "Ya'ua son of Omri" — and appears, perhaps in person, kneeling on the Black Obelisk among his tribute-bearers with their silver and gold. It is a striking case of a biblical king surfacing in the enemy's own records.

Keil and Delitzsch attend to the Hebrew. The command to "carry him to an inner chamber" is literally "a chamber in a chamber," the innermost room — a phrase that recurs at 1 Kings 20:30, which Thenius took as a mark of common authorship. "From among his brethren" they gloss, with Ellicott, as his comrades in arms, his fellow-captains; the young prophet must single Jehu out from the company and get him alone.

The unifying note, once more, is secrecy. Ellicott states the object plainly — "the object was secrecy" — and every commentator reads the innermost chamber, the private summons, and the coming order to flee as of a piece. The kingdom is to be transferred by a real, God-authorized act, yet one performed out of sight, its validity to be proved not by witnesses but by the sudden animation of the man anointed. What the Lord means to do openly in Israel he sets in motion behind a closed door.
3:
This is the heart of the errand — "take the box of oil, pour it on his head, and say, Thus saith the LORD, I have anointed thee king over Israel" — and though the source here is thin, the witnesses draw out three things from the few words. First, the Reformed John Gill notes the plain circumstance the verse assumes: the pouring is to be done "when in the chamber together alone," the act itself matching the secrecy commanded in the verses before.

Second, Charles Ellicott observes that only "the chief part of the message to Jehu is here given, to avoid publicity"; the fuller commission — the charge to cut off the house of Ahab — is withheld until it can be spoken privately, and is reported at length only when Jehu repeats it (vv.6–9). The narrative is itself practising the discretion it describes.

Third, Ellicott weighs a small textual matter with a real point behind it. The Hebrew here and at v.12 reads literally "king unto Israel," but "a great number of manuscripts, and all the versions in both places, read over Israel." The difference is slight, yet the preferred reading underlines the truth the whole scene turns on: Jehu is not merely raised up in Israel but set over Israel, and set there by God. The closing "tarry not," he adds, is meant "to avoid all questioning, and to give greater force to the act" — the anointer must vanish, so that the deed stands alone, unattested by any human witness and needing none.

Taken together these notes let even so brief a verse carry the chapter's governing paradox. The formula "Thus saith the LORD, I have anointed thee king" is the language of open, public investiture — the very words by which prophets had made kings before Israel's eyes. Yet every instruction around it presses toward concealment and haste. The witnesses agree that the two hold together: the authority is wholly divine and therefore needs no crowd to ratify it, while the timing is wholly prudent, sparing both the messenger and the moment from premature exposure. God's word makes the king; man's applause is not required.
4:
The narrative repeats itself deliberately — "So the young man, even the young man the prophet, went to Ramoth-gilead" — and the witnesses divide, mildly, over that doubled and awkward phrase. John Gill takes the repetition as emphasis, and reads it to the young man's honour: it is remarked that a young man went, "who was more fit for this service than Elisha," partly for his age and strength, partly because he "would be less known"; and it was "a bold and daring action in a young man to anoint a new king," as well as an honourable one. Gill notes further that he was "not only one of the sons of the prophets, but was a prophet himself, though young" — the Targum softening this to "a disciple of the prophets."

The grammatical-historical voices suspect a textual snarl rather than a point of substance. Adam Clarke would translate not "the young man the prophet" but "the servant of the prophet" — that is, Elisha's attendant, the minister who now filled the place once held by Gehazi. Charles Ellicott agrees the construction is "unusual," and records that some manuscripts, the Septuagint, and the Syriac simply omit the second "the young man," yielding the smoother "So the young man, the prophet, went."

The disagreement is small and friendly, and it does not touch the sense: whether we call him prophet or prophet's servant, a youth carried the flask to Ramoth-gilead and did there a king-making deed. What the witnesses will not let pass unnoticed is the fitness of the choice. The errand needed speed, obscurity, and nerve — qualities of the young — and it needed a man under authority, sent and instructed, who would pour the oil, speak the word, and flee without lingering for reward.

In the very phrasing that the copyists found hard, Gill hears the Spirit's own stress on the lowly and unlikely instrument. A dynasty steeped in blood, propped by Baal's prophets and Jezebel's court, is about to fall — and the hand that sets its fall in motion is a nameless youth from a prophetic school, carrying nothing but a flask of oil and a word from God. The greatness of the errand and the smallness of the errand-boy are held together on purpose, and the witnesses will not have us miss it.
5:
The scene shifts to the officers' quarters, and the witnesses picture it with care. "The captains of the host were sitting" — either, says John Gill, "at a table, being at dinner, or at a council of war." Charles Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch agree they sat in council with Jehu, in the court of the house, when the young prophet strode in with his abrupt word: "I have an errand to thee, O captain."

The small drama the commentators savour is Jehu's reply, "Unto which of all us?" Nothing is made of it here as evasion; rather it fits the impression, developed across the chapter, of a man who does not grasp at honour but lets it come to him. Keil and Delitzsch follow the action closely: on hearing that the word was for him, "the latter rose up and went with him into the house, i.e., into the interior of the house, in the court of which the captains were sitting together." There, they add, looking ahead, "the pupil of the prophets poured oil upon Jehu's head, and announced to him that Jehovah had anointed him king for Israel, and that he was to smite... the house of Ahab, to avenge upon it the blood of the prophets" (1 Kings 18:4; 19:10).

The verse is a hinge, and the witnesses treat it as such. The instructions of the opening verses are now being executed to the letter: the messenger has reached Ramoth, found the captains, singled out Jehu from among his brethren, and drawn him aside. The public setting — a room full of generals — makes the coming privacy all the more pointed; the errand is announced before them all but delivered to one man alone behind the door.

Ellicott notes simply that he "went into Jehu's headquarters" — the ordinary business of a war-camp becoming, unbeknown to the rest, the antechamber of a revolution. The soldiers see only that a wild-looking youth wants a private word with their commander; they do not yet know that a kingdom is turning on it. The witnesses do not divide here; they agree that the machinery of the divine commission is grinding forward exactly as ordered, and that the very ordinariness of the scene — captains at their meal, a messenger at the door — is the disguise under which God moves.
6:
Now the oil is poured and the word spoken: "Thus saith the LORD God of Israel, I have anointed thee king over the people of the LORD, even over Israel." The witnesses dwell on that title, the people of the LORD. Adam Clarke hears in it a charge as well as a dignity: it "pointed out to Jehu that he was to rule that people according to God's law," and therefore "to restore the pure worship of the Most High in Israel." Charles Ellicott draws out the theology: "Israel being Jehovah's people, Jehovah was Israel's true king, and therefore it was within His sovereign right to appoint whom He would as His earthly representative." The anointing does not create a rival throne; it names a deputy under the real King of Israel.

Ellicott also marks a longer arc, noting that "the commission to Elijah (1 Kings 19:16) was thus fulfilled by his successor" — the charge once laid on Elijah to anoint Jehu is discharged here by Elisha's messenger. That very point provokes the chapter's one clash with modern criticism. Ellicott reports the skeptic Reuss, who conjectured that the whole anointing story was "substituted in the present narration for what another document related of Elijah," and who suggested there is "no need to try to clear Elisha of the charge of being a revolutionary and a regicide," since a new dynasty would use the prophet's name to legitimise itself just as the houses of Kish and Jesse used Samuel's. Ellicott dismisses this as "pure conjecture" — it is easier to suppose that Elijah had instructed his successor to carry out the commission — and closes with dry rebuke: Reuss "is being considerably wiser than our only authorities."

Keil and Delitzsch, meanwhile, lay out the sweep of the sentence now pronounced: the whole house of Ahab shall perish; Jehovah will "avenge the blood of my servants the prophets, and the blood of all the servants of the LORD, at the hand of Jezebel"; the house shall be made like the houses of Jeroboam and Baasha (1 Kings 21:21–23); the dogs shall eat Jezebel. Then the young prophet "opened the door, and fled."

So the witnesses agree on what has happened and what it means. A private act in an inner room has invested Jehu with a public office, and invested it as a sacred trust: he is set over God's own people, bound to rule by God's law and to execute God's long-deferred judgment. Against the suspicion that this is merely a prophet meddling in politics, Ellicott speaks for the tradition — the deed is the discharge of a divine commission, not the plotting of a king-maker.
7:
The commission now names the deed and its ground, and the witnesses close ranks on both. First the deed: "thou shalt smite the house of Ahab thy master." John Gill feels the moral weight — for Jehu to strike his own sovereign's house would, on any ordinary reckoning, be "high treason," "unlawful and criminal"; what makes it just is the express "order for it from the King of kings, and Lord of lords." Charles Ellicott sharpens the object: not "Ahab thy master" but "the house of Ahab thy lords," the whole guilty dynasty.

Then the ground, and here the Wesleyan voices speak most pointedly. John Wesley presses what the verse does not say: "That they were idolaters was bad enough: yet that is not mentioned here." The controversy God has with the house of Ahab "is for being persecutors" — for the blood of the prophets and of "all the servants of the LORD" shed at Jezebel's hand. Wesley draws the terrible maxim: "Nothing fills the measure of the iniquity of any prince so as this doth, nor brings a surer or sorer ruin." Adam Clarke anchors the sentence in the primal law of blood: "for their most cruel murders they have forfeited their own lives, according to that immutable law, He that sheddeth man's blood by man shall his blood be shed" (Genesis 9:6).

Ellicott supplies the history behind the charge, pointing to Jezebel's slaughter of the prophets (1 Kings 18:4, 13) and observing that though it is nowhere else narrated, it is "in itself probable" that she "persecuted to the death those who clung to the exclusive worship of Jehovah."

So the witnesses converge on a sober theology of judgment. Jehu is no free agent avenging himself or clearing his path to power; he is an executioner under warrant, and the warrant rests not first on Ahab's idolatry but on Ahab's house having made itself a murderer of God's servants. That is the crime, Wesley insists, that God requites most surely — and Clarke, that the shedding of innocent blood forfeits the shedder's own. The relationship of servant to master, which would otherwise forbid the act, is overruled by a higher allegiance: Jehu owes more to his Master in heaven than to his master Ahab.
8:
The doom is now stated in its fullest reach: "the whole house of Ahab shall perish." John Gill gives the plain sense — every branch is to be "cut off by death, and that in a violent manner, not one should escape." The verse allows no remnant, no survivor to carry the tainted line forward, and the witnesses feel the totality of it.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott lingers over the vivid, deliberately coarse Hebrew idiom for that completeness. The phrase "him that is shut up and left in Israel" is a proverbial way of saying everyone without exception — bond and free, the guarded and the abandoned alike. Ellicott notes how the French of Reuss even imitates the original's alliteration, "qu'il soit caché ou lâché en Israel," to catch its sweeping, rhyming finality; and he flags the small textual variants — "shall perish" against "I will cause to perish" — that ride on differing Hebrew points but do not alter the sense. For the rest of the verse's language he simply refers the reader back to the parent oracle in 1 Kings 21:21, and to its earlier echo against the house of Jeroboam in 1 Kings 14:10.

That backward reference is the whole point the witnesses want pressed. This is not a fresh sentence but the ratification of one already pronounced by Elijah years before over Ahab in Naboth's vineyard. What was threatened is now being commissioned; what God said He would do, He now sends a man to do. The very crudeness of the idiom — sparing no image of the utter sweeping-away of a royal house — matches the seriousness of the offence that provoked it.

And so even this brief verse teaches the witnesses' steady lesson about divine justice: it is patient but not forgetful. Between the crime and this hour lay a long delay, in which Ahab's line went on reigning as though the curse had lapsed. It had not. When the measure of the iniquity was full, the old word woke and demanded its whole payment — "the whole house," down to the last. God's judgments may sleep, but they do not die.
9:
The sentence reaches for precedents: "I will make the house of Ahab like the house of Jeroboam the son of Nebat, and like the house of Baasha the son of Ahijah." John Gill, whose note here is only a cross-reference, points the reader back to the same threat as first uttered; the witnesses read the verse chiefly as a summons to memory. Two earlier dynasties of the northern kingdom had already been rooted out for their sins, and Ahab's house is now told it shall share their fate exactly.

Keil and Delitzsch observe that these verses "are simply a repetition of the threat in 1 Kings 21:21–23" — the words Elijah spoke over Ahab returning almost unchanged. Charles Ellicott, for his part, fills in the historical pointers: for the fall of Baasha's line he directs us to 1 Kings 16:3–4, and for Jeroboam's to the earlier oracle, adding the antiquarian note that Shalmaneser II mentions a king of Ammon also named Ba'sa — a reminder that these were common Semitic names.

The force of the comparison is what the witnesses want felt. To be made "like the house of Jeroboam" and "like the house of Baasha" is to be handed a documented verdict, not a vague threat. Jeroboam's house had been utterly cut off (1 Kings 15:29); Baasha's likewise (1 Kings 16:11–12); and in both cases the extermination fulfilled a prophet's word to the letter. Ahab's house is thus shown its own future in a mirror already hung on the wall of Israel's history.

There is no debate among the witnesses here, and little for them to expound; the verse is a hinge of memory and warning. Yet the very economy of it carries a moral the commentators are content to let stand: the God who judged Jeroboam and Baasha has not changed, and the pattern of their ruin is the pledge of Ahab's. Sin in high places writes the same ending again and again, and the reappearance of the old formula is itself a sermon on the unwearied consistency of divine justice.
10:
The sentence ends where its horror is most concentrated: "the dogs shall eat Jezebel in the portion of Jezreel, and there shall be none to bury her." John Gill notes that this too is no new thing but a threat already spoken, the Targum reading "the inheritance of Jezreel" for the field where it would fall. Then, with a curt "and he opened the door, and fled," the young prophet is gone, his errand discharged, leaving Jehu alone with the weight of what he has heard.

The Wesleyan Adam Clarke fastens on the one thing that makes this line unforgettable — its precision as prophecy. "How most minutely was this prophecy fulfilled!" he exclaims, sending the reader forward to the grim scene at the chapter's close (vv.33–37), where every clause comes true: Jezebel thrown down, trodden under, devoured by the dogs, and left without a grave. Charles Ellicott adds only the literal Hebrew order — "and Jezebel the dogs shall eat" — and refers it back to Elijah's original threat in 1 Kings 21:23.

The witnesses do not need many words, because the verse's power lies in the reader's foreknowledge. Spoken here in a shut chamber at Ramoth, it hangs over the rest of the chapter like a sentence awaiting execution. The queen who had shed the blood of prophets, who had ordered Naboth stoned outside the city and seized his ground, is told the manner and the very place of her end — the same portion of Jezreel her house had stolen.

That exactness is the point the commentators press. To deny Jezebel burial and give her body to the dogs was, in the ancient world, the ultimate degradation, the erasure of a name. And it would happen not by accident but by the word of the Lord, uttered years in advance through Elijah and repeated now through a nameless disciple. The witnesses let the coming fulfilment do the preaching: what God threatens, He performs, down to the field, the dogs, and the missing grave. Between the promise and the deed stands only the working-out of the chapter — and the God who does not forget.
11:
Jehu rejoins his fellow-officers, and their first word sets the tone: "Wherefore came this mad fellow to thee?" The witnesses seize on that sneer. Keil and Delitzsch explain the Hebrew meshugga, "a madman," flung at the prophet "in derision, with reference to the ecstatic utterances of the prophets." Matthew Henry turns it into a meditation on the world's abiding contempt for godliness. The captains called him mad "because he was one of those that would not run with them to an excess of riot" (1 Pet. 4:4) but "lived a life of self-denial, mortification, and contempt of the world." Henry gathers the parallels: they said of the Saviour, "He is beside himself"; of John the Baptist, "He hath a devil"; of Paul, that "much learning hath made him mad" (Acts 26:24). "The highest wisdom," Henry concludes, "is thus represented as folly, and those that best understand themselves are looked upon as beside themselves" — a note he grounds in Hosea's own complaint that "the prophet is a fool, the spiritual man is mad" (Hosea 9:7).

Adam Clarke presses the irony harder against the soldiers themselves: "In vain do such pretend that they fight for religion... if they persecute religion and scoff at holy men." Was the prophet reckoned mad "because he was a holy man of God"? That, Clarke says drily, "has been an old custom with all the seed, the sons, of the serpent." John Wesley agrees they "perceived him to be a prophet by his habit, and gestures, and manner of speech," and yet, being "prophane soldiers," "esteemed the prophets mad-men."

Then comes Jehu's guarded reply, "Ye know the man, and his communication," and here the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott finds two possible readings, hearing an emphasis on the ye. Either Jehu implies "the man was sent to him by his fellow-generals — that they had planned the whole thing," and he is probing their disposition; or, "more probably," he means simply, "Why ask me, when you yourselves must have divined the right answer?" Clarke reads it as deliberate concealment: "Jehu did not appear willing to tell them what had been done, lest it should promote jealousy and envy." Henry takes it as a gentle rebuke — "You know the man to be a prophet, why then do you call him a mad fellow?"

Under the by-play the witnesses agree on two things. The scoffing name is a badge of the world's enmity to God's messengers, worn before by Christ and His apostles; and Jehu's evasion shows a man weighing his moment, "not afraid to own" his commission yet unwilling to broach it until pressed. Henry's summary stands for them all: those who "faithfully deliver the Lord's message to sinners have in all ages been treated as madmen" — while it is the ungodly who "are mad indeed."
12:
The captains refuse to be put off. "It is false, tell us now," they press, and the witnesses probe both their retort and Jehu's disclosure. John Gill reconstructs the psychology: they did not believe Jehu was giving them the whole truth, "which they concluded to be of some importance, by the hastiness of the messenger, the privacy between them, the countenance of Jehu, which discovered thoughtfulness and concern, and the trifling answer he sought to put them off with" — every clue sharpening their curiosity until they grew "urgent and importunate to know the truth."

Over the single Hebrew word rendered "It is false" the grammatical-historical commentators refine the sense. Charles Ellicott thinks "It is false" too strong; the captains reply to Jehu's "Oh, you know all about it!" with one word — "trickery!" — meaning "you are pretending," "mere evasion." Adam Clarke reports the Chaldee's blunter "Thou liest," but allows the gentler paraphrase: "We know he has said nothing but folly and lies, nevertheless, let us hear what he has said." Keil and Delitzsch read it plainly as sheqer, "thou dost not speak truth."

Whichever tone we hear, the effect is the same, and Ellicott names it: their very insistence "convinced Jehu that he might safely trust them." He had probed their disposition with an evasion; their eager pressing answered his probe. So he tells them straight — "Thus and thus spake he to me, saying, Thus saith the LORD, I have anointed thee king over Israel."

The witnesses do not divide over this swift exchange; they read it as the hinge on which the conspiracy swings from secret to open. Jehu had received his commission behind a shut door, its validity resting on no witness but himself. Now, tested by his comrades' curiosity and reassured of their loyalty, he risks the disclosure — and the risk is real, for he knew, as Matthew Henry observed, that some might "out of loyalty to Joram or envy of him... crush his interest in its infancy." That he speaks anyway the commentators trace to faith in the divine appointment: "he relied on the divine appointment, and was not afraid to own it, knowing whom he had trusted." The word once spoken cannot be recalled, and the army's response will be immediate.
13:
At the disclosure the officers act instantly, and the witnesses read their homage with delight — and one long philological puzzle. "They hasted, and took every man his garment, and put it under him on the top of the stairs, and blew with trumpets, saying, Jehu is king." Adam Clarke reads the gesture as a coronation ceremony of acknowledgement, and hears in it a foreshadowing: "it was by such a ceremony that the multitudes acknowledged Jesus Christ for the Messiah and King of Israel, a little before his passion" (Matthew 21:7; Luke 19:36). The act says, in Clarke's paraphrase, "As we put our garments under his feet, so we place every thing under his authority." John Wesley adds the eastern manners: it was done "in token of reverence to his person, that they would not have his feet to touch the ground, and that they put themselves and their concerns under his feet."

The famous crux is the phrase "on the top of the stairs" — Hebrew gerem hammaaloth — over which, as Keil says, "the early translators have done nothing but guess." Three readings contend. The Targum, followed by the rabbis and by Clarke, takes it of a public sundial, a flight of graduated steps such as the dial of Ahaz (2 Kings 20:11), whose topmost platform made a fit stage for the proclamation. Keil and Delitzsch, with Kimchi, render "upon the steps themselves," that is, "upon the bare steps." Charles Ellicott, weighing that gerem elsewhere means "bone" or, in Arabic, "body," proposes "on to the body of the stairs" — the external stairway to the roof serving as "an extemporised throne, or rather platform, for the king" (2 Kings 11:14).

John Gill leans to the sundial too, "the degree of hours... on which were engraven the twelve hours of the day," while insisting the garments spread were "not their wearing apparel, but carpets, or pieces of tapestry." Ellicott catches the tempo from the Septuagint's likely original — "they heard, and hasted" — reading in the "instantaneous action of the generals" the proof "that there must have existed a strong feeling against Joram in the army and an enthusiasm for Jehu which only required a word from him to precipitate a revolution."

The debate over the steps is real but does not touch the substance, and the witnesses know it. Whether sundial, bare stair, or rooftop platform, the meaning of the moment is one: an army, at a word, lays its cloaks beneath a new king and blows the trumpet. What had been whispered in an inner chamber is now shouted over the ranks, and the revolution the prophet set in motion is public and irreversible.
14:
The narrator pauses to name the deed for what it is — "So Jehu... conspired against Joram" — and then, in a circumstantial aside, reminds us of the war that set the stage. The witnesses read the verse as both a legal label and a tactical hinge. John Gill states the plain fact: Jehu "and the captains with him entered into a confederacy to depose Joram." The word conspired is not softened; yet, as the earlier verses have established, this conspiracy carries a warrant no ordinary rebellion could claim.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch unpack the parenthesis: Joram "had been keeping guard at Ramoth in Gilead," defending the city against Hazael's attacks, and had gone back to Jezreel to be healed of his wounds. This, they stress, shows "the Israelites were in possession of the city of Ramoth, and were defending it against the attacks of the Syrians" — so the verb "kept" cannot mean a siege of the town. Adam Clarke reads the same military situation: the confederate armies "appear to have taken this city," but had to "watch their conquests," since Hazael was "determined to retake it if possible."

Then the tactic. Jehu's first order upon resolving to strike is not a march but a blockade of news: "If it is your wish, let no fugitive go from the city, to announce it in Jezreel." Keil explains the aim precisely — that word of the conspiracy or the proclamation should not outrun Jehu to the wounded king. The revolution's whole hope lay in surprise.

The witnesses do not contend over this verse; they let its two halves illumine each other. The public label — conspiracy — is set beside the secret precaution — let none escape — and both are held within the frame of the divine commission already given. Jehu is at once a rebel by the world's reckoning and an executioner by God's; and his shrewdness in sealing the city, far from marking mere ambition, serves the swift, unresisted fall of a house already sentenced. The war with Syria that had drawn Joram away and left Jehu in command now becomes, under Providence, the very opening through which judgment enters.
15:
The verse completes the parenthesis begun before it: "King Joram was returned to be healed in Jezreel of the wounds which the Syrians had given him," and then Jehu speaks — "If it be your minds, then let none go forth nor escape out of the city to go to tell it in Jezreel." John Gill notes only that the fact of Joram's wounding "is here repeated" to fix the setting. The grammatical-historical commentators do the rest.

Charles Ellicott presses the point Keil had urged, and makes it decisive: "Let none go forth" is literally "let not a fugitive go forth," and "this proves that Ramoth was in the hands of the Israelite army. If they were besieging the city, as Josephus relates, Jehu's command is unintelligible." One cannot forbid deserters to slip out of a town one is attacking from without; the order only makes sense if Jehu holds the town and controls its gates. So a single military command quietly settles a disputed point of history against Josephus.

Ellicott also lingers on the tender opening phrase, "If it be your minds" — literally "if it be your soul," a courteous, almost deferential appeal to the captains' will, which the Vulgate rightly renders si vobis placet, "if it please you." The commentators note how it fits the shrewd Jehu already glimpsed: he binds the officers to him by consulting them, making the conspiracy their own act as much as his.

Beneath the tactics the witnesses see the design. Joram lies at Jezreel, unarmed and unaware, healing from Syrian arrows; his ablest general holds the frontier fortress and its gates; and between the two lies a day's ride that Jehu means to cross before any warning can. The very wound that sent the king home to recover has left him exposed, and the very city he defended has become the base from which his own captain moves against him. What looks like careful generalship is, in the witnesses' reading, the outworking of a judgment long decreed — every circumstance falling into place to deliver Ahab's house into the appointed hand.
16:
"So Jehu rode in a chariot, and went to Jezreel; for Joram lay there. And Ahaziah king of Judah was come down to see Joram." The witnesses set the stage for the double judgment about to fall. John Gill pictures Jehu riding "in great pomp and majesty as a king." Matthew Henry supplies the geography and the tension: from Ramoth-gilead to Jezreel "was more than one day's march," the Jordan to be crossed midway, and Jehu "marched with all possible expedition," taking "the utmost precaution to prevent the tidings from getting to Jezreel before him." The reader watches him come "within sight first, and then within reach, of the devoted king."

The presence of Ahaziah is what the witnesses mark as fateful. Charles Ellicott reminds us he "was come down" on the friendly errand recorded earlier (2 Kings 8:29), to visit his wounded kinsman; neither king suspects a thing. Henry draws the sombre providence: "How strangely has Providence sometimes ordered it, that men have been in haste to meet their ruin when their day has come to fall." The two royal houses, of Israel and of Judah, joined by marriage and now by iniquity, are gathered in one place at the one hour appointed for their reckoning.

Ellicott also does the textual spade-work, untangling a knot in the Greek. The Septuagint here carries an expanded reading — "Joram king of Israel was being healed in Jezreel of the shots wherewith the Arameans shot him..." — which he judges to be a marginal note on the previous verse, "inadvertently inserted by some transcriber," together with a stray gloss, "because he was mighty and a man of might," that had originally belonged against "Hazael king of Syria." It is a small window on how such texts grew, and on the care needed to read them.

The witnesses do not divide; they build a single scene of dramatic irony. Jehu drives hard across Jordan under a warrant of judgment; Joram lies healing, expecting news of the war; Ahaziah lingers as a guest. All the pieces the earlier verses set out — the sealed city, the wounded king, the visiting ally — now converge on Naboth's ground, where, as the next verses show, the watchman's cry will begin the end.
17:
"There stood a watchman on the tower in Jezreel, and he spied the company of Jehu as he came." The witnesses attend to the sentinel, the size of the approaching band, and the king's first uneasy question. John Gill explains the watchman's post: set to see "afar off when an enemy was coming," he was "now on his watch tower" the more because "the king was there." Adam Clarke widens the picture to the ancient system of signals — watchmen "fixed on elevated places and generally within hearing of each other" served "as a kind of telegraph, to communicate intelligence through the whole country" (2 Samuel 13:34).

Charles Ellicott weighs the word for the "company," shiph'ah, which "literally means overflow, and so a multitude" — of waters, camels, or horses. Jehu, he infers, "was accompanied, therefore, by a considerable force," not a lone rider; a troop was pouring toward the city.

The disputed note is the tone of Joram's "Is it peace?" Ellicott insists the king "is not yet apprehensive"; his question "merely means, What is the news?" — he is expecting word from the army at Ramoth, and sends "a horseman to meet them, and let him say, Is it peace?" Against this Ellicott records Thenius's reading, "Come ye with friendly or hostile intention?" — but he counters shrewdly: had Joram truly feared hostility, "would the king have sent a single horseman to ascertain the truth?" The lone messenger betrays a mind still at ease.

The witnesses build the suspense together. From the tower a swelling company is seen; the king, unsuspecting, wants only the news from the front; and so he dispatches one man to ride out and ask the question that will echo, with mounting force, through the verses ahead — "Is it peace?" The reader, who knows what Jehu carries, hears in the innocent inquiry the first tremor of a doom the king cannot yet imagine. Every detail — the height of the tower, the size of the troop, the single horseman — is arranged by the narrator to hold Joram a moment longer in the false calm before the storm.
18:
The first messenger rides out: "Thus saith the king, Is it peace?" And Jehu answers with a rebuff that the witnesses find full of cold command: "What hast thou to do with peace? turn thee behind me." Charles Ellicott calls it "a rough evasion" — "What business is it of yours, on what ground I am come?" — and reads the whole exchange as a study in Jehu's self-possession: "Conscious of his strength, Jehu can despise the royal message, and the messenger durst not disobey the fierce general, when ordered summarily to the rear."

The purpose of the rebuff, the commentators agree, is control of information. Ellicott states it plainly: "Of course Jehu wished to prevent an alarm being raised in Jezreel." Every rider Joram sends out to gather news Jehu simply absorbs into his own train, so that the king learns nothing and the surprise is preserved. Keil and Delitzsch render the taunt exactly — "what hast thou to do with peace?" i.e. "to trouble thyself about it" — and "turn behind me," meaning "to follow me."

Then the narrator returns to the tower: "And the watchman told, saying, The messenger came to them, but he cometh not again." John Gill notes only that the horseman, reaching the company, "was detained, as the other" would be. The single fact — a messenger who rides out and does not ride back — is itself the news, though Joram does not yet read it aright.

The witnesses let the scene work by its ominous economy. There is no violence yet, only a question turned aside and a man swallowed up in the advancing column. Jehu's curt "turn thee behind me" does two things at once: it enlists Joram's own servant into his following, and it withholds from the palace the one word — peace, or no peace — on which everything hangs. The reader feels the net drawing tight. What the king means as a query about the distant war is answered, unknowingly, by the very silence of his returning messengers; and the more he sends, the more the truth is kept from him, until he must at last go out himself.
19:
"Then he sent out a second on horseback, which came to them, and said, Thus saith the king, Is it peace? And Jehu answered, What hast thou to do with peace? turn thee behind me." The scene repeats almost word for word, and the witnesses read the repetition itself as the point. John Gill observes simply that the second rider "did the same as the first messenger, and had the same answer, and was bid to do the same" — that is, to fall in behind Jehu.

Charles Ellicott tidies a small textual matter with a real bearing on the mood. The ordinary Hebrew of Jehu's report gives the messenger's word as a bare salutation, "Peace!"; but Ellicott, with the versions, many editions, and some manuscripts, reads it as the question "Is it peace?" — and explains why the point matters: "Joram is still unsuspicious of evil. Some accident might have detained his first messenger." The king, that is, does not yet suspect treachery even when one rider fails to return; he assumes a mishap and simply sends another.

That is the whole force of the verse for the commentators — a second innocent probe swallowed up exactly as the first. The doubling is not idle narration but the slow tightening of dramatic irony. Two of the king's men have now gone out and neither has come back, yet Joram reads the silence as delay rather than danger. Each unanswered "Is it peace?" leaves him more exposed and less informed, and draws the unstoppable column one stage nearer his gate.

The witnesses, having little text to expound, let the pattern preach. It shows a doomed man clinging to the hope of ordinary news while the extraordinary bears down on him; and it shows Jehu's discipline, refusing to be drawn into premature disclosure, patiently absorbing every emissary. The repeated question, unanswered and unheeded, becomes a kind of refrain of judgment — peace demanded again and again by a house that had filled the land with blood, and each time turned silently aside. There is, the commentators imply, a fearful mercy even in the delay: the king is given moment after moment in which to read the signs aright, and squanders each one. Two horsemen have vanished into the coming host, and still the palace waits for good news that will never arrive.
20:
The watchman speaks a third time, and now the news has a name: "He came even unto them, and cometh not again: and the driving is like the driving of Jehu the son of Nimshi; for he driveth furiously." The witnesses fasten on that vivid detail — the recognizable, headlong style of the man at the reins. Adam Clarke makes it a portrait: "Jehu was a bold, daring, prompt, and precipitate general," whose manner of driving "was almost proverbial," so that he could be known from afar by the very fury of his approach.

Charles Ellicott does the philology and finds a pointed connection: the word rendered "furiously" is related to the very word for "mad fellow" thrown at the prophet earlier (v.11) — "Jehu's chariot swayed unsteadily as he drove madly on." There is a grim irony in it: the derided madness of the prophet and the reckless madness of the driver are named by kindred words, and both are instruments of the same divine purpose. Ellicott records too the alternative tradition — the Targum and Josephus render the opposite sense, "quietly," Josephus even having Jehu drive "rather slowly, and in orderly fashion" — a reading he traces to a confusion of Hebrew words.

Matthew Henry draws the character-lesson the detail invites, and draws it with balance. On the one hand, "a man of such a violent temper was fittest for the service to which Jehu was designated"; "the wisdom of God is seen in the choice of proper instruments to be employed in his work." On the other, Henry will not flatter the trait: "it is not much for any man's reputation to be known by his fury. He that has rule over his own spirit is better than the mighty."

So the witnesses hold two truths together over this small verse. God fits the instrument to the task, and Jehu's impetuous energy is exactly what the swift, unhesitating execution of judgment requires. Yet impetuosity is no virtue in itself; a man remembered chiefly for driving furiously is not thereby commended. The watchman's report identifies the coming judgment by its very recklessness — and warns, in Henry's hands, that zeal in a good cause is still not the same thing as self-mastery.
21:
"And Joram said, Make ready. And his chariot was made ready. And Joram king of Israel and Ahaziah king of Judah went out, each in his chariot... and met him in the portion of Naboth the Jezreelite." The witnesses read the fatal step the two kings take, and above all the ground on which they take it. Charles Ellicott renders the command literally — "bind," that is, the horses to the chariot — and draws the psychological point: "Had he suspected treachery, he would hardly have left the shelter of the walls of Jezreel, and ventured forth without a guard." Joram rides out unarmed and unescorted, merely "curious to know why his messengers had not returned." Adam Clarke agrees the kings "had no suspicion of what was done at Ramoth-gilead; else they would not have ventured their persons as they now did."

But it is the place that the witnesses find charged with meaning. They meet Jehu "in the portion of Naboth the Jezreelite" — the very field Ahab had coveted and Jezebel had murdered to obtain. John Wesley catches the terrible fitness: "The very sight of that ground was enough to make Jehu triumph and Joram tremble." And he states the principle the coincidence teaches — one the commentators return to again and again in this chapter — "The circumstances of events are sometimes so ordered by Divine providence, as to make the punishment answer the sin, as face answers face in a glass."

Ellicott adds the topographical note that Naboth's vineyard "now formed part of the pleasure-grounds of the palace" (1 Kings 21:16), so that the king, riding out from his own gate, could hardly avoid the spot; the scene of the old crime lay at his very door.

The witnesses do not divide; they let providence do the preaching. Joram sets out in ignorance, drawn by nothing more sinister than curiosity, and is guided — as men are, when their day has come — straight to the ground where his family's guilt cries loudest. He has no idea he is keeping an appointment made years before in Elijah's curse. What he means as a short ride to meet a returning general is, in the witnesses' reading, a summons he cannot refuse, to the one field in all Israel where his house stands most plainly condemned.
22:
The two chariots meet, and Joram calls out the question he has asked all along: "Is it peace, Jehu?" Now at last it is answered, and the answer is a thunderclap: "What peace, so long as the whoredoms of thy mother Jezebel and her witchcrafts are so many?" The witnesses gather around two things — the meaning of Jezebel's "whoredoms," and the great principle that there is no peace for the wicked.

On the "whoredoms and witchcrafts" the commentators divide, but gently. Adam Clarke takes them in "their common and most obvious sense," judging that Jezebel "was the patroness and supporter of a very impure system of religion," so that literal debauchery is meant, not merely idolatry. John Wesley allows both readings — the words may be understood "either literally," since "spiritual whoredom, which is idolatry," was often joined with corporal, "or spiritually, of her idolatry, which is often called whoredom, because it is a departing from God." Charles Ellicott leans to the spiritual: "whoredoms" in "the spiritual sense, i.e. idolatries," and "witchcrafts" the sorceries and spells "common among Semitic idolaters," forbidden in the Law (Exodus 22:18; Deuteronomy 18:10–11).

All agree on why Jehu names the mother rather than the son. Wesley: "He mentions not Joram's, but his mother's sins; because they were more notorious," and "the principal cause why God inflicted... these judgments." Ellicott sees Jehu here assuming "the part of champion of the legitimate worship against Jezebel and her foreign innovations, and the lawless tyrannies by which she sought to enforce them."

The pastoral point the witnesses land on together is the ringing "What peace?" It is, as Wesley says flatly, the truth that "the way of sin can never be the way of peace." So long as the whoredoms and witchcrafts "continue" — Ellicott's careful rendering — no peace is possible; the very question exposes the folly of a house that had cried peace to itself while its guilt mounted unrepented. Jehu, who once dared not have spoken so plainly, now deals honestly with the king, and his blunt refusal of peace is not cruelty but truth: there can be no peace with God, no peace of conscience, no peace in the land, while the sin that provoked heaven is cherished and defended.
23:
"And Joram turned his hands, and fled, and said to Ahaziah, There is treachery, O Ahaziah." In an instant the king reads everything in Jehu's answer, and the witnesses trace both his sudden understanding and his cry of warning. Keil and Delitzsch follow the Hebrew: "Joram detecting the conspiracy from this reply, turned round" — "he turned his hands," the same idiom used of Ahab's charioteer wheeling about at Ramoth (1 Kings 22:34) — "and fled, calling out to Ahaziah mirmah, deceit, i.e., we are deceived, in actual fact betrayed." Charles Ellicott renders the shout as two bare words flung over the shoulder — "Guile, or fraud, Ahaziah!" — as the king wrenched his horses round to fly.

Adam Clarke marks the human touch: "This was the first intimation he had of it," and even now, in his own peril, "he feels for the safety of his friend Ahaziah." The warning is not only for himself; the doomed king cries out to spare his guest. John Gill pictures the physical act — Joram "taking hold of the horses' reins" to turn them, or directing the charioteer, "whither he fled."

The witnesses read the moment as the pivot of the whole encounter. Up to this line Joram has clung to the language of peace; Jehu's single sentence about Jezebel's whoredoms has stripped that hope away, and the king knows at once that the general before him is not a returning subordinate but an executioner. His flight is instinctive and useless.

The word he chooses — treachery — is, in the witnesses' hearing, both true and blind: true, in that he is indeed betrayed by his own captain; blind, in that what looks to him like mere human treason is in fact the long-appointed judgment of God, from which no turning of the reins can carry him away. He flees the wrong danger, naming a plot when he should have named the curse of Elijah now come home. The tragedy of Ahab's house is concentrated in that misreading: to the very end it sees the sword of man and never the hand of God behind it.
24:
"And Jehu drew a bow with his full strength, and smote Jehoram between his arms, and the arrow went out at his heart, and he sunk down in his chariot." The witnesses attend to the marksmanship, the wound, and the theology of the shot. On the Hebrew idiom for the drawing, the grammatical-historical readers refine the English. Adam Clarke corrects the phrase: it is literally "He filled his hand with a bow" — that is, "He immediately took up his bow, set his arrow, and let fly." Charles Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch weigh the same expression: Keil, "filled his hand with the bow," i.e. seized it; Ellicott noting the idiom "to fill a bow" means to stretch it (Zechariah 9:13). Either way, Jehu shot with deliberate, full-drawn force.

The wound is exactly described. "Between his arms," all agree, means between the shoulders — for Joram had turned to flee, and the arrow took him from behind, driving obliquely through so that it "went out at his heart," and he "sunk down in his chariot." Keil and Delitzsch spell it out: struck "in his back between the shoulders in an oblique direction, so that the arrow came out at his heart." It was a killing shot, and the king died on the spot.

The Wesleyan John Wesley lifts the moment from tactics to providence in a single stroke: the shaft was "one of God's arrows, which he ordained against the persecutor" (Psalms 7:13). The bow is Jehu's; the aim, the witnesses insist, is God's. What reads as a soldier's shot at a fleeing man is, in their eyes, the descent of a sentence long prepared — the arrow that Scripture pictures God ordaining against those who will not repent.

The witnesses do not divide here; they read the death of Joram as the first and topmost branch of Ahab's house cut off, precisely as the commission required. Matthew Henry observes that "he was now the top branch of Ahab's house, and therefore was first cut off," dying "a criminal, under the sentence of the law." The very body will now be dealt with according to that sentence, as the next verses show; for the field into which it falls is no accident, but the appointed place where Ahab's guilt is repaid.
25:
Joram dead, Jehu turns to his officer with a command steeped in memory: "Then said Jehu to Bidkar his captain, Take up, and cast him in the portion of the field of Naboth the Jezreelite: for remember how that, when I and thou rode together after Ahab his father, the LORD laid this burden upon him." The witnesses draw out the man addressed, the shared memory, and the weight of the word burden. John Gill clarifies the relationship: Bidkar is "not Joram's, but Jehu's captain, though he had been the former's, and his father Ahab's also" — an old comrade from the days when both rode in Ahab's train.

Charles Ellicott savours the name. The Syriac gives Bar-dekar, "son of stabbing," that is, "stabber" or "slayer" — "a very suitable name for Jehu's squire" — and he explains the title "captain" as adjutant or aide-de-camp. Keil and Delitzsch unpack the shared recollection: "remember how we, I and thou, both rode... behind his father Ahab, and Jehovah pronounced this threat upon him"; the two of them had been present, riding in attendance, on the day Elijah met Ahab.

The theologically loaded word is burden. Keil notes that massa means "a burden, then a prophetic utterance of a threatening nature," and Ellicott renders the clause accordingly: "Jehovah uttered this prophetic utterance upon him," pointing back to "the oracle uttered by Elijah against Ahab when taking possession of Naboth's vineyard" (1 Kings 21:17–29).

The witnesses read the command as an act of conscious fulfilment. Jehu does not merely dispose of a corpse; he stages it. He casts the body into the stolen field on purpose, and calls his fellow-rider to witness that the two of them heard, years before, the very word now coming true before their eyes. What Elijah spoke as a threat while Ahab gloated over Naboth's ground, Jehu now executes on Ahab's son upon that same ground — and he wants it remembered, named, and understood as the Lord's doing, not his own. The scene is deliberately turned into a testimony: prophecy heard on that spot, judgment done on that spot, and an old soldier summoned to certify that God keeps His word.
26:
Jehu now quotes the oracle itself: "Surely I have seen yesterday the blood of Naboth, and the blood of his sons, saith the LORD; and I will requite thee in this plat." The witnesses concentrate on two puzzles the words raise — the mention of Naboth's sons, and the precise "yesterday" — and on the marks of authenticity in the utterance.

The sons are the crux. The account in 1 Kings 21 records only Naboth's own death, not his sons'. Adam Clarke reasons from motive that they must have perished too: since Ahab and Jezebel wanted the vineyard "entirely, and for ever," they would hardly "leave any of his posterity, who might at a future time reclaim it," and Jezebel's contrivance to have Naboth "convicted of treason and atheism" was designed precisely so that "his whole family might be involved in his ruin." Charles Ellicott agrees the killing of the sons is "neither stated nor implied" in the earlier chapter, but finds no difficulty: it "would be quite in accordance with ancient practice to slay the sons of one accused of blasphemy along with their father" (compare Achan, Joshua 7:24–25). He accounts for the difference between the narratives by their sources — the present is "the exact version of an eye-witness, viz., Jehu himself," while the record in Kings came "from a less direct source."

The "yesterday" the commentators take pointedly. Ellicott reads it to mean "Ahab seized the vineyard the day after the murder of Naboth," a detail not given before; the blood was scarcely dry when he took possession. He parses the opening "Surely" as the remnant of an emphatic oath — "If I have not seen, may I perish" — its original force long worn smooth by usage.

Finally the witnesses note the diction that stamps the saying as genuine prophecy. Ellicott observes that "saith the LORD" is literally "is the thing uttered of Jehovah," a phrase "uncommon except in the writings of the prophets," which together with the word "burden" of the previous verse "establish the historical authenticity of the short oracle of Elijah." Its very brevity and solemnity, he adds, "would stamp it ineffaceably upon the memory of those who heard it." So the closing charge — "take and cast him into the plat of ground, according to the word of the LORD" — is fulfilment done knowingly, blood requited on the very ground where blood was shed.
27:
The judgment now overtakes the king of Judah: "But when Ahaziah the king of Judah saw this, he fled by the way of the garden house. And Jehu followed after him, and said, Smite him also in the chariot." The witnesses handle the flight, the wound, and above all the famous discrepancy between this account and Chronicles. Charles Ellicott renders Jehu's order with its true urgency — not the flat "smite him also," but "Him too! shoot him in the chariot!" — and traces the route: Ahaziah fled toward "the garden house," was struck "at the going up to Gur, which is by Ibleam," and fled on to Megiddo, where he died.

The knot is that 2 Chronicles 22:8–9 tells the story differently — Ahaziah hiding in Samaria, caught, brought to Jehu, and slain. Adam Clarke meets it by quoting John Lightfoot's full harmonization: Jehu slays Joram, Ahaziah flees and hides in Samaria, Jehu comes there and has him found and brought, then orders him carried "up towards Gur, by Ibleam," and slain — "The story in the book of Kings is short, but the book of Chronicles shows the order." John Wesley offers the same reconciliation in brief: Ahaziah "fled first to Megiddo, and thence to Samaria, where he was caught, and thence brought to Jehu, and by his sentence was put to death at Megiddo."

Ellicott, more cautious, will not force the two into one. He judges "the definite assignment of localities in the present account" to be "a mark of greater trustworthiness," bluntly calls the Rashi-and-Keil style of harmonizing "revolting to common sense," and thinks it "better to assume a corruption of the text in one or the other narrative." Here the witnesses genuinely differ in method: Clarke and Wesley harmonize by combining, Ellicott by suspecting a scribal slip.

Yet on the meaning of Ahaziah's death they are one, and Matthew Henry speaks for them. Ahaziah "would not have been slain but that he was joined with the house of Ahab both in affinity and in iniquity. He was one of them" — made so "by his sins" — "and therefore he must fare as they fared." Henry adds the sober warning the episode presses on every reader: "It is a dangerous thing to associate with evil-doers; we may be entangled both in guilt and misery by it." The grandson of godly Jehoshaphat dies in the ruin of Ahab's house because he had made that house his own.
28:
"And his servants carried him in a chariot to Jerusalem, and buried him in his sepulchre with his fathers in the city of David." Amid the slaughter, Ahaziah alone receives an honourable burial, and the witnesses ask why. John Gill supplies the reason that moved Jehu to permit it: it was done "with the leave of Jehu, because he was the grandson of Jehoshaphat, a sincere worshipper of God." The godliness of the grandfather earns the grandson a grave; the memory of Jehoshaphat's faithfulness shelters even a son of Ahab's alliance from the utter disgrace that will fall on Jezebel.

The grammatical-historical commentators fill in the details of the entombment. Charles Ellicott renders the phrase "in his sepulchre" as "in his own sepulchre, which he had in his lifetime prepared, according to the custom of antiquity" — the king had made ready his tomb, and to it his body is now brought. Ellicott and the versions note that the Hebrew almost demands a supplied verb, "and brought him," after "made him ride"; the servants set his corpse on the chariot and bore it the long way south to the city of David.

The contrast the witnesses feel is with what is coming. Two kings have died this day; one, Joram, is flung into Naboth's field as a criminal under sentence; the other, Ahaziah, though caught in the same judgment for the same guilt of association, is carried tenderly to lie "with his fathers." The difference is not in their innocence — Ahaziah too "was one of them" by his sins — but in the lingering mercy shown for a pious ancestor's sake.

So the verse teaches, quietly, that God remembers the righteous even in the judgment of their descendants. Jehoshaphat had walked with the Lord, and three generations later that fidelity purchases his grandson a decent burial when all around is ruin. The commentators do not press the point into a doctrine, but they let it stand as a gleam of grace in a dark chapter: the same providence that repays Ahab's blood in Naboth's field also honours Jehoshaphat's faith in a Judahite tomb, distinguishing, even here, between the house that persecuted the prophets and the house that, for all its later folly, had once served God.
29:
"And in the eleventh year of Joram the son of Ahab began Ahaziah to reign over Judah." The verse is a chronological footnote, and the witnesses' whole task is to square it with the earlier notice that dated his accession to Joram's twelfth year (2 Kings 8:25). None treats the difference as a real contradiction.

John Gill resolves it by the turn of the year: the accession fell "at the close of the eleventh, and the beginning of the twelfth"; or else Ahaziah "began to reign with his father in the eleventh... and in the twelfth" alone, when his father was dead. Adam Clarke reads it as a co-regency followed by a sole reign: "Then he began to reign as viceroy to his father in his sickness" (2 Chronicles 21:18–19), "but in Joram's twelfth year he began to reign alone" (2 Kings 8:26).

Charles Ellicott is the most relaxed of all. "Such a difference is not remarkable," he writes, "inasmuch as the synchronisms between the reigns of the two kingdoms are not based upon exact records"; and "different computations might make the same year the eleventh or twelfth of Joram." He adds the candid aside that the verse is "a parenthesis, and perhaps spurious" — a scribal insertion, he suspects, rather than part of the original narrative.

The witnesses thus offer two complementary answers: a real co-regency (Clarke, Gill) or a mere difference of reckoning between the two kingdoms' loose synchronisms (Ellicott). Neither shakes their confidence in the text. The point they are content to make is a general one about the chronology of Kings: its double bookkeeping of Israel's and Judah's reigns, counted now from an accession, now from a co-regency, now inclusively and now exclusively, readily yields such small divergences without any real conflict. A verse that might trouble a hasty reader becomes, in the commentators' hands, an occasion to explain how ancient regnal dating worked — and a reminder that apparent numerical clashes in Scripture usually dissolve once the manner of counting is understood. It is worth noting, too, that the commentators feel no anxiety in raising the difficulty openly; they neither hide it nor strain to force an artificial harmony, but set the possibilities side by side and let the reader judge, confident that a book so careful in its cross-dating is not overthrown by a single year's reckoning.
30:
The scene shifts to Jezreel and to the most famous death in the chapter. "And when Jehu was come to Jezreel, Jezebel heard of it; and she painted her face, and tired her head, and looked out at a window." The witnesses read the queen's toilet as an act of defiance, and debate its motive. Matthew Henry sets the frame: this was "the greatest delinquent in the house of Ahab" — she who "introduced Baal, slew the Lord's prophets, contrived the murder of Naboth" — and now, her day come to fall, she meets it not with mourning but with paint. "Instead of hiding herself, as one afraid of divine vengeance, she exposed herself to it and scorned to flee." Henry sees her painting and dressing as an attempt "to appear like herself... great and majestic," hoping "to daunt Jehu, to put him out of countenance, and to stop his career."

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott offers a nobler reading of the same act: "Jezebel put on her royal apparel in order to die as a queen," and he sets beside her the dying Cleopatra calling for her crown and best attires. He firmly rejects the theory of Ewald (after Ephrem Syrus) "that Jezebel thought to captivate the conqueror by her charms," pointing out that she was the grandmother of a grown king and that "Oriental women fade early." Adam Clarke, meanwhile, turns antiquarian, explaining at length how she "set her eyes in paint" with the antimony kohl still used across the East to enlarge and darken the eye — the very cosmetic the prophets elsewhere name (Jeremiah 4:30; Ezekiel 23:40).

Henry draws the moral from her hardness. She had time and warning to repent, "but never did any thus harden their hearts against him and prosper"; and he finds a type of her in the Jezebel of Thyatira, given "space to repent" yet ripening for ruin (Revelation 2:20–23). "There is not a surer presage of ruin," Henry warns, "than an unhumbled heart under humbling providences."

Whether we read the paint as a bid to intimidate (Henry), to die with royal dignity (Ellicott), or — rejected by all — to seduce (Ewald), the witnesses agree on what it reveals: a soul utterly unbowed before God's judgment, meeting the sword with cosmetics and scorn. The Lord had "called to baldness and girding with sackcloth" (Isaiah 22:12–13); Jezebel answered with painting and dressing, "walking contrary to God" to the last.
31:
As Jehu enters the gate, Jezebel flings down her challenge: "Had Zimri peace, who slew his master?" The witnesses unfold the taunt and its studied insolence. Charles Ellicott reads it as "an ironical greeting" that turns her captors' own question back on Jehu — "Art well, thou Zimri, his master's murderer?" She brands him "a second Zimri," a regicide like the man who slew Baasha's son and then reigned only seven days (1 Kings 16:15–18), hinting "that she herself is now the sovereign to whom Jehu owes an account." John Wesley catches the sting compactly: "Remember thy brother traitor Zimri had but a very short enjoyment of the benefit of his treason."

Adam Clarke records the range of ancient renderings. Jarchi paraphrased the words to conciliate rather than provoke — "If thou hast slain thy master, it is no new thing; for Zimri also slew Elah" — while most versions take them as bitter irony, "Health to Zimri, the slayer of his master!" Matthew Henry, who gives the verse its fullest treatment, weighs her use of the precedent and finds it self-condemning. She "took no notice of the hand of God gone out against her family," but "flew in the face of him that was only the sword in his hand." She threatened Jehu with Zimri's fate, yet "the instance of Zimri was misapplied": Zimri "had no warrant for what he did," being driven "merely by his own ambition and cruelty," whereas Jehu "was anointed by one of the sons of the prophets, and did this by order from heaven, which would bear him out."

Henry draws the discriminating lesson: "In comparing persons and things we must carefully distinguish between the precious and the vile, and take heed lest from the fate of sinful men we read the doom of useful men." The parallel that Jezebel presses is precisely the one that does not hold — for the whole difference between a Zimri and a Jehu is the difference between self-willed treason and a divine commission.

So the witnesses hear in her defiant proverb the last flourish of an unrepentant will. She meets the executioner not with confession but with a threat, and with a threat that recoils upon her, since it rests on ignoring the very thing that distinguishes God's avenger from a mere usurper. To the end she reckons with the sword of man and refuses to see the hand of God, and her misapplied warning becomes, in the commentators' reading, a mirror of her own doom.
32:
"And he lifted up his face to the window, and said, Who is on my side? who? And there looked out to him two or three eunuchs." The witnesses read Jehu's refusal to trade words with the queen and his swift appeal to her own attendants. Charles Ellicott observes that Jehu "did not deign to answer the worthless woman"; he simply looks up and issues his challenge. Nor, Ellicott adds, does the cry imply that Jezebel "had made preparations for resistance," as Thenius thought; rather, "Jehu knew that the imperious and cruel queen was well hated by the palace officials."

That hatred is the hinge. The "two or three eunuchs" who a moment before "had crouched in servile dread before Jezebel," Ellicott notes, "would now be eager to curry favour with the regicide, and, at the same time, wreak their malice upon their former tyrant." John Gill marks only that Jehu lifted up his face "on hearing the above words" — turning from her taunt to the men behind her. Ellicott finds the doubled cry, "Who is on my side? who?", to accord "well with Jehu's character," the impatient, headlong energy the chapter has stressed throughout.

The witnesses draw out the terrible isolation of the moment. Jezebel has ruled Israel through three reigns, feared and obeyed; yet at the decisive hour she has not one friend within her own household. The chamberlains who served her turn instantly at a stranger's word. Her power, it appears, was propped entirely by fear, and when a stronger fear arrived, it collapsed in an instant.

Here the moral the commentators find is the emptiness of tyranny. A cruel greatness commands no loyalty; it purchases only servile dread, which flees or turns the moment it is safe to do so. Jehu's "Who is on my side?" — an echo, elsewhere, of Moses at the camp and the Psalmist against the evil-doers — needs no army to answer it; the queen's own servants suffice. The verse shows judgment falling not merely from without but from within, as the very household Jezebel had cowed becomes the instrument of her fall.
33:
Jehu's order is instant and merciless: "Throw her down. So they threw her down: and some of her blood was sprinkled on the wall, and on the horses: and he trode her under foot." The witnesses record the violence plainly and note a small point of grammar. Adam Clarke does not soften it: "What a terrible death! She was already, by the fall, almost dashed to pieces; and the brutal Jehu trampled her already mangled body under his horse's feet." Keil and Delitzsch describe the eunuchs, at Jehu's word, throwing "the proud queen out of the window, so that some of her blood spirted upon the wall and the horses, and Jehu trampled her down, driving over her with his horses and chariot."

The grammatical nicety concerns who did the trampling. Charles Ellicott notes that "all the versions have they — i.e. the horses — trode," which Thenius supposed were "excited by the blood being sprinkled upon them"; but the Hebrew's "he trode her under foot," Ellicott judges, "plainly means, he drove over her fallen body." He checks the excess of some interpreters: Ewald "goes beyond the text in stating that Jehu spurned her with his own feet." The picture is grim enough without embellishment — the queen flung down, her blood spattering wall and chariot-team, her body crushed under the wheels.

The witnesses read the scene as exact, deliberate retribution. Matthew Henry had already named the correspondence: "Thus was vengeance taken on her for the stoning of Naboth" — she who had a righteous man cast down and stoned is herself cast down and broken. The very manner of her death answers the crime that most defined her reign.

And so the horror serves the chapter's steady theme. This is not wanton cruelty running loose but the working-out of a sentence long pronounced; the blood on the wall is the seal of Elijah's word. Clarke's revulsion and Ellicott's careful grammar together keep the reader from both sentimentality and exaggeration: what happened was brutal, and what happened was just — "and say," as Henry bids, "The Lord is righteous."
34:
"And when he was come in, he did eat and drink, and said, Go, see now this cursed woman, and bury her: for she is a king's daughter." The witnesses find in this cool sequence — dinner first, then a thought for burial — a revealing glimpse of Jehu. Charles Ellicott reads it with candour: "Savage warrior as he was, he forgot all about the victim of his violence until he had appeased the demands of his appetite. Then he could remember that even Jezebel was of royal rank, and perhaps a touch of remorse may be discerned in the mandate for her burial." Keil and Delitzsch gloss the phrase "this cursed woman" as "the woman smitten by the curse of God."

The commentators weigh the reason Jehu gives — "she is a king's daughter." Adam Clarke lists her lineage in full: "daughter of the king of Tyre; wife of Ahab, king of Israel; mother of Joram, king of Israel;... and grandmother of Ahaziah, king of Judah." John Wesley catches a pointed omission in Jehu's wording: "he doth not say, because she was a king's wife, lest he should seem to shew any respect to that wicked house of Ahab, which God had devoted to utter destruction." Jehu will honour her birth but not her marriage; the dignity he grants reaches back to Tyre, not to Ahab's throne.

Wesley also notes the irony that Jehu "had forgot the charge given him above" (v.10) — that the dogs should eat Jezebel and none bury her. The executioner, having just fulfilled so much prophecy, momentarily forgets the very clause that forbids what he now orders.

So the witnesses read the verse as a study in the mixture of the man. Jehu is God's instrument, zealous and thorough; yet he is also a hard soldier who dines while the body lies in the street, and a politician who measures out a calculated respect. The half-remorse, the careful phrasing, the forgotten command — all show a man doing God's work with very human hands. That his order to bury her cannot be carried out, the next verse will reveal, is itself the Lord's overruling: what Jehu forgot, God remembered, and the word that Jezebel should have no grave stands, whatever her killer belatedly intends.
35:
"And they went to bury her: but they found no more of her than the skull, and the feet, and the palms of her hands." Jehu's belated order to bury the queen cannot be obeyed, and the witnesses mark the grim fulfilment. Keil and Delitzsch state the plain fact: "when they went to bury her, they found nothing but her skull, the two feet, and the two hollow hands. The rest had been eaten by the dogs and dragged away." What Jehu had forgotten, God had not: the sentence that the dogs should eat Jezebel was already accomplished while he ate and drank within.

Adam Clarke presses the exactness of the prophecy: "How literally was the prediction... fulfilled!" — the word spoken through Elijah, "The dogs shall eat Jezebel by the wall of Jezreel" (1 Kings 21:23), now come true to the letter. He passes on the curious rabbinic conceit of Jarchi and Kimchi, that the dogs spared just the skull, feet, and palms "because in her festal dances she danced... on her hands and feet" — a fancy Clarke reports without endorsing, adding only that Jezebel "was, no doubt, guilty of the foulest actions."

The witnesses read the scene as the crown of the chapter's theme of fulfilled prophecy. Every clause of the old oracle is honoured. The queen who had shed Naboth's blood by the wall of Jezreel is devoured by the wall of Jezreel; the woman who had painted her face to appear majestic is reduced to a skull; the hands and feet that had wrought and walked in wickedness are all that remain of her royal person.

And the timing is the sermon. Between Jehu's command to bury and the servants' return, the dogs had done their work — so that the burial he intended became impossible precisely because the word of the Lord had run ahead of him. The commentators let the ghastly inventory speak: what God threatens, He performs, exactly and irreversibly, and no belated human courtesy can undo it. The remnant of Jezebel is not a tragedy of chance but the visible seal of a prophecy that could not fail.
36:
The servants return with their report, and Jehu names what has happened: "This is the word of the LORD, which he spake by his servant Elijah the Tishbite, saying, In the portion of Jezreel shall dogs eat the flesh of Jezebel." The witnesses read the verse as the explicit tying of deed to prophecy. Keil and Delitzsch note that Jehu, on hearing the report, declares "this has been done in fulfilment of the word of the Lord" spoken through Elijah (1 Kings 21:23).

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends to a small but real textual difference between the oracle as quoted here and as first recorded. Where this verse reads "in the portion (heleq) of Jezreel," the earlier text of Elijah's word reads "by the wall (hel) of Jezreel" — a difference, Ellicott judges, "due to the loss of the final letter," a scribal slip, "not an original difference, as Keil assumes." It is a characteristic instance of the commentators' care: even in so solemn a moment they weigh the exact letters, and disagree — Keil taking the two words as genuinely distinct, Ellicott as a copyist's error.

Yet the disagreement is technical and touches nothing of substance. Portion or wall, the meaning stands: in the very ground of Jezreel, where Naboth was murdered and his vineyard seized, the dogs have eaten Jezebel, exactly as foretold. John Gill simply records the report and Jehu's recognition of the prophecy fulfilled.

The witnesses read Jehu's declaration as the interpretive key he himself presses upon the scene. He will not let the horror pass as mere carnage; he labels it, as he had labelled Joram's death in Naboth's field, "the word of the LORD." Twice in this chapter the executioner pauses to certify that what he does is not his own vengeance but the accomplishment of a prophet's sentence long deferred. The commentators take him at his word: the death of Jezebel is not an accident of revolution but the exact discharge of a promise God made through Elijah, and had never forgotten. There is, they observe, a further irony in the speaker: it is Jehu, no saint but a bloody and half-obedient soldier, who preaches the sermon over her corpse, so that even the executioner is made to confess that the hand at work in Jezreel was not his own but the Lord's.
37:
The prophecy is rounded out with its most desolate clause: "the carcase of Jezebel shall be as dung upon the face of the field in the portion of Jezreel; so that they shall not say, This is Jezebel." The witnesses gather to the total erasure of a name. Adam Clarke rises to eloquence: because her body "was not buried under the earth, but was eaten by the dogs, this saying was also literally fulfilled." "Though so great a woman by her birth, connections, and alliances, she had not the honour of a tomb! There was not even a solitary stone to say, Here lies Jezebel." Clarke closes on the awe of judgment: "Judgment is God's strange work; but when he contends, how terrible are his judgments!"

Charles Ellicott weighs whether this closing sentence belongs to Elijah's original oracle. Against Keil, who took it as "a free expansion" supplied by Jehu, Ellicott holds it "probably original," a genuine portion of the prophecy though not recorded in the earlier chapter (1 Kings 21:23). He notes that Luther, differently again, read the verse as a closing remark of the historian rather than a continuation of the oracle. The sense of the last clause he fixes precisely: "men will no longer be able to recognise her mangled remains."

The commentators do not miss the bitter aptness of her name. John Gill and Clarke both observe that Jezebel was thought to mean "Where is dung?" — so that her end as "dung upon the face of the field" answers, with awful irony, the very sound of the name she bore. Matthew Henry had drawn the same close: her name should remain nowhere "but as stigmatized in sacred writ"; none could point and say, "This is Jezebel's dust... or This is Jezebel's grave." "Thus the name of the wicked shall rot" — rot, Henry adds, "above ground."

So the chapter ends where its sentences began, on the exact correspondence of word and deed. The witnesses read the whole grim close as one sustained proof that God keeps His threatenings as surely as His promises. Jezebel had sought, by paint and defiance, to make her end a queenly spectacle; instead she becomes an unnameable smear on a stolen field, her memory preserved only as a byword of judgment. Against every attempt of the wicked to secure a lasting name, the commentators set this last line: what God has cursed, no monument can rescue, and the only immortality left to Jezebel is her infamy.
